sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives, university tropical-fruit extension (Florida and Hawaii), Queensland and Western Australian government horticulture, and Australian nursery sources.

core.intro: Rollinia (Annona mucosa, once called Rollinia deliciosa, and also known as biriba or Brazilian custard apple) is a fast-growing Amazonian cousin of the custard apple, prized for soft, creamy flesh that tastes like lemon meringue pie. It is an ultra-tropical tree: it wants real warmth, high humidity and freedom from frost, and its fruit is so perishable that it is almost always a backyard tree rather than a market crop. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply wherever rollinia will grow.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Most rollinia sold in Australia is a seedling, grown from seed because grafted material has long been scarce. Seedlings are reasonably true to type and crop well, but they vary in fruit size and quality and take a little longer to bear, so propagating from superior trees is worthwhile. A handful of named grafted selections now exist from local collectors: Picone, a Northern NSW selection with large juicy fruit and a reputation as a reliable cropper; Limberlost, a far north Queensland selection that bears heavily; and Sputnik, noted for a bright yellow, lemony fruit. There are no formally recognised cultivars worldwide, so treat these as nursery selections rather than fixed varieties. Match your choice to the named trees in the in-stock table above.

sections.heading: Pollination, and why one tree is enough
sections.body: You only need a single rollinia to get fruit, but it helps to understand its flowers. Like other annonas, each flower opens female first and turns male later (a habit called protogynous dichogamy), and a whole tree often does this in step, so a flower rarely pollinates itself and natural fruit set can be low. Small beetles and flies do the work, drawn by the sweet, ripe-custard scent, and warm, humid, still weather brings them out and keeps the flowers receptive. If your tree flowers well but sets little, hand pollinate: collect pollen from male-stage flowers in the afternoon and dust it onto the open, female-stage flowers early next morning with a small brush. This is the same trick that lifts crops on cherimoya and atemoya.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give rollinia the warmest, most sheltered, frost-free spot you have. It grows in full sun or part shade and is not fussy about soil type, but it must have rich, free-draining ground with plenty of organic matter, so mound the planting site if your soil is heavy clay. The roots are shallow and the wood is brittle, so a young tree blows over easily: shelter it from strong wind, keep the lower branches to anchor and shade the root zone, and if it does go over, lay it down and let it recover rather than hauling it upright, which tears the roots. Container-grown trees go in best once the soil has warmed (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water, feeding and humidity
sections.body: Rollinia likes steady moisture all year and resents both extremes: a dry spell in the warm months makes it drop leaves, flowers and fruit, while waterlogged ground rots the roots and can kill it, so water often but never let the tree sit wet. Humidity matters too, because dry air works against fruit set, which is one reason it thrives in the humid tropics. Feed it through the warm growing season with a balanced fertiliser two or three times, backed up with organic matter such as poultry manure, blood and bone and a light mulch kept clear of the trunk. Watch the leaves for hunger: rollinia is prone to zinc deficiency (stunted shoots and yellowing), corrected with a spring foliar spray of zinc sulphate, and to iron-linked yellowing in cold spells or on limey soils, where a chelated iron feed helps.

sections.heading: Harvest and eating
sections.body: Rollinia runs from flower to ripe fruit in roughly five months. Cut the fruit from the tree with secateurs as the skin turns from green to yellow, before the soft conical spines start to blacken, because the fruit bruises if you pull or drop it. It then ripens on the bench over three to six days and is ready when it gives to gentle pressure without the skin breaking. Eat it within a day or two of that point: once ripe it softens fast, the spines darken, and the flesh turns almost to a sweet liquid before it begins to ferment. That short window is why rollinia barely travels and is best eaten fresh at home, scooped from the skin or blended into a drink. A seedling usually fruits in three to four years, a grafted tree in two to three.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Decide between a seedling and a grafted selection. A seedling is cheaper and crops reasonably true, but it is slower and a little unpredictable; a grafted, named tree such as Picone or Limberlost fruits sooner and to a known type. Whichever you buy, the make-or-break question is your climate: rollinia needs a genuinely warm, humid, frost-free position, so be honest about whether you can give it one before you spend the money. If you like the family, its close cousin the custard apple (atemoya) is hardier and easier in more of Australia. The in-stock table above shows the named trees available now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Is rollinia the same as a custard apple?
faqs.a: They are close relatives, not the same fruit. Both belong to the annona family, but rollinia (Annona mucosa, also called biriba or Brazilian custard apple) has its own knobbly, cream-yellow fruit and a distinctive lemon meringue flavour, while the tree sold simply as custard apple in Australia is usually the atemoya. Rollinia is the more tropical and more perishable of the two.

Soft, sweet and slightly tart, with a creamy, almost custard texture that many people compare to lemon meringue pie, which is why it is sometimes called the lemon meringue fruit. You eat the white flesh with a spoon and leave the many dark seeds, much as you would with its custard apple cousins.

faqs.q: How big does a rollinia tree get, and how quickly?
faqs.a: It is a vigorous grower that can put on a metre or more a year and reach 8 metres or taller if left alone, but under cultivation it is usually held to about 4 to 6 metres with pruning, which also keeps the fruit in reach and steadies a top-heavy, shallow-rooted tree against the wind. In cyclone country growers keep it lower still.

faqs.q: Can I grow rollinia in a pot?
faqs.a: For a few years, yes. A large container (35 litres or more) suits a young tree and lets you stand it in the warmest, most humid corner and move it under cover in a cold or wet snap, which is the only way many cooler-climate gardeners keep one alive. It grows fast and fruits far better in the ground, though, so a pot is a holding pattern rather than a permanent home.

QLD.intro: Growing rollinia in Queensland. Queensland is the one part of Australia where rollinia is genuinely at home: the warm, humid, frost-free tropics of the far north grow it as a backyard treasure, on both the wet coast and the cooler elevated Tablelands.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rollinia wants heat, humidity and no frost, and the far north delivers all three. It crops well on the humid tropical coast and, as the original North Queensland growers found, just as happily in the cooler air of the elevated Tablelands. Frost is the hard limit: even a light frost, down around 3 degrees on the grass, can kill a young tree, so cold inland hollows are out and the trees need the warmest, most sheltered position you can give them.

sections.heading: Where rollinia grows in QLD
sections.body: The stronghold is the wet tropics of the far north: the coastal belt around Cairns, Innisfail, Tully and Cardwell, and the highland country of the Atherton Tablelands, including Julatten and Mareeba, where some of Australia's first rollinia trees fruited. The Rare Fruit Council's far north growers around the Cardwell and Johnstone River district kept it going for decades. Further south it becomes a backyard rarity rather than part of the landscape.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: In the far north rollinia tends to flower around January and February, set fruit through March and April, and ripen its main crop about five months later, from late winter into spring (roughly August to October), with scattered fruit at other times of year in the warmest spots. Cut each fruit as the skin yellows and let it finish ripening indoors.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth and humidity bring the usual annona troubles. Queensland fruit fly stings ripening fruit and lists custard apple among its hosts, so net or bag fruit as it nears ripe. Spotting bugs pierce young fruit and shoots, causing dimples and drop. Mealybugs and scale shelter among the soft spines and on the leaves, often tended by ants. Anthracnose, a fungal disease, blackens fruit and leaves in long wet, humid spells, so keep the canopy open and airy and clear away fallen fruit.

faqs.q: Can you grow rollinia in Brisbane or the Gold Coast?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree in a warm, sheltered, frost-free corner of a southeast Queensland garden, with summer humidity and steady water, but the cooler subtropical winters make fruit less reliable than in the tropical north. Protect young trees from any cold snap, choose the hottest microclimate you have, and treat a good crop as a bonus.

faqs.q: Why is rollinia so hard to buy as fruit in Queensland?
faqs.a: Because it does not travel. The ripe fruit softens to almost a liquid within a day or two and bruises at a touch, so it cannot be trucked to market like a custard apple. Almost all of it is grown in home gardens and shared fresh among far north growers, which is exactly why planting your own tree is the only dependable way to taste it.

NSW.intro: Growing rollinia in New South Wales. Rollinia is a tree for the warm, frost-free far north coast of NSW and effectively nowhere else in the state, with the subtropical Northern Rivers its southern foothold.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rollinia needs warmth, humidity and freedom from frost, and only the frost-free coastal pockets of the far north come close. Even there the cooler subtropical winters make it more of a backyard novelty than a dependable crop, and it sets and ripens fruit less reliably than in the tropics. The tablelands and the inland are out of the question, because frost cuts down young trees. It is telling that the Picone selection, one of the better local rollinias, came from a Northern NSW collector working in just such a frost-free spot.

sections.heading: Where rollinia grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers is the state's subtropical fruit belt and the only realistic home for rollinia: the warm, humid country around Murwillumbah, the Tweed, Lismore and Alstonville. This is the southern edge of annona country in Australia, where even the hardier custard apple peters out, and south of the Coffs coast the winters turn too cool for a tropical annona to bother with.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Like its custard apple relatives, rollinia here ripens in the cooler half of the year, mostly across autumn and into winter, and the cooler subtropical position tends to push the crop later and make it shorter and less certain than a tropical season. Cut the fruit as the skin turns yellow and ripen it indoors, and expect a modest backyard pick rather than a steady supply.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: The warm, humid coast that lets rollinia grow also feeds its pests. Queensland fruit fly is established along the NSW coast and stings ripening fruit. Spotting bugs damage young fruit and shoots. Anthracnose is the main disease in the humid Northern Rivers air, blackening fruit in wet weather, and the Alstonville district is a known hot spot for it on custard apple, so an open canopy and tidy clean-up of fallen fruit earn their keep.

faqs.q: How far south can you grow rollinia in NSW?
faqs.a: Realistically only the frost-free far north coast. A tree may survive in a warm, sheltered Sydney microclimate, but it rarely ripens fruit there, and anywhere with regular frost (the tablelands, the cold inland and most of the south) will kill it. The further south of the Northern Rivers you go, the more it becomes a foliage curiosity rather than a fruit tree.

faqs.q: What can I grow instead if I am south of the warm coast?
faqs.a: Step down to a hardier exotic. A sheltered, frost-free spot on the mid-north or central coast may still suit the custard apple (atemoya), and for genuinely cool or frosty gardens the feijoa gives you a tough, subtropical-tasting fruit that shrugs off the cold rollinia cannot survive.

WA.intro: Growing rollinia in Western Australia. Rollinia is a hard ask in WA, because the tree wants tropical heat and high humidity together, and WA tends to offer one without the other, so here it is a collector's challenge rather than a backyard fruit tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The humid tropics rollinia loves do not really exist in WA. The frost-free north, around Carnarvon, the Gascoyne and the Kimberley, has the heat but is semi-arid and dry, where tropical fruit lives entirely on irrigation. Perth and the south-west have a Mediterranean climate, with dry summers and cool, sometimes frosty winters that a tender tropical tree dislikes. Either way you end up working against the weather, propping up humidity with shelter, heavy mulch and constant water.

sections.heading: Where you might grow it in WA
sections.body: Your best shot is a warm, sheltered, frost-free and well-watered microclimate. The Carnarvon and Gascoyne irrigation districts grow tropical fruit such as mangoes and bananas, and a sheltered Perth courtyard with overhead watering to lift the humidity can keep a rollinia alive, but reliable fruit anywhere in WA is a stretch. Hardier annona relatives are a far surer bet in the same gardens.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: WA has Mediterranean fruit fly rather than the Queensland fruit fly of the east, and it is established across the south-west and much of the north (though not the Ord irrigation area at Kununurra). Like all fruit flies it stings soft, ripe fruit, and rollinia's thin-skinned, super-soft fruit is an easy target, so net or bag it as it ripens.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from the eastern states must be permitted and are usually treated and certified before entry, so many interstate nurseries will not post here. The in-stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can legally ship a tree to WA, which in practice is a short list.

faqs.q: Can you grow rollinia in Perth?
faqs.a: Only as a challenge. A hot, sheltered, frost-free corner, heavy mulch and overhead watering to raise the humidity can keep a tree going, and a greenhouse helps, but Perth's dry summers and cool winters make a reliable crop unlikely. Most gardeners here will get more fruit, with far less fuss, from a hardier subtropical.

faqs.q: Which rare fruits crop more easily than rollinia in WA?
faqs.a: Plenty do. In the cool south-west a feijoa is tough and dependable, and in the warm, irrigated north mangoes and the hardier custard apple are far surer than a humidity-hungry rollinia. Save rollinia for when you have a genuinely warm, humid, frost-free spot to spare.

VIC.intro: Growing rollinia in Victoria. Rollinia is not a Victorian fruit tree. The state is too cool and too frost-prone for a tree that a single light frost can kill, so here it is a heated-greenhouse curiosity rather than a backyard crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Rollinia needs a long, warm, humid and frost-free year to carry its fruit through the roughly five months from flower to ripe. Victoria's cool-temperate climate and winter frosts are the opposite of that, and even the much hardier custard apple does not crop south of the northern NSW coast, so an outdoor rollinia in Victoria would be cut down by the first real cold snap long before it ever fruited.

sections.heading: If you still want to try
sections.body: Grow it in a large pot in a warm greenhouse, conservatory or sunroom, keep it above about 5 degrees all winter, and accept that you will mostly get handsome foliage rather than fruit. For a hardy exotic that actually crops outdoors in a Melbourne garden, the feijoa is a far better choice, and rollinia is best enjoyed as fruit when you travel north.

faqs.q: Will rollinia fruit outdoors in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Almost certainly not. The tree is unlikely even to survive a Melbourne winter outdoors, let alone ripen fruit, because frost kills it and the warm season is too short to carry a crop. This far south it is a heated-greenhouse plant, grown for novelty and foliage rather than for the fruit.

faqs.q: What should I grow instead of rollinia in Victoria?
faqs.a: None of the tropical annonas crop reliably this far south, so for that soft, sweet, custard-textured fruit your best outdoor option is a hardy substitute like the feijoa, which is fully frost-tough. Otherwise, buy rollinia fruit when you are travelling through the tropics.
